messages.content: My husband got a job in another city, so I had to leave this position. check grammar
The grammar in this sentence is correct.
im applying for beauty advisor, in cv the reason why i left classroom management  and became a content creator was i got interested in creating educational app. can you help me?

Sure! Here is a possible way to phrase your explanation in your CV for leaving classroom management to become a content creator:

"I left my previous position in classroom management because I had a newfound interest in creating educational apps. As a content creator, I was able to pursue my passion and hone my skills in digital content development and design. I am now excited to apply my creativity, communication, and customer service skills to excel as a beauty advisor."

I left my previous position in classroom management because I was interested in creating educational apps. As a content creator, I was able to pursue my passion and develop my skills in digital content development and design. check grammar
The grammar in this sentence is correct.
We moved to another city, because my husband got a job. change it formally
"We relocated to another city due to my husband's employment opportunity."
Mrs. Mr diference

"Mrs." is used for a married woman, while "Mr." is used for a man. "Mrs." indicates that the woman is married and has taken her husband's last name, while "Mr." is used for any man regardless of his marital status.
Ms Mrs different

"Ms." and "Mrs." are both titles used for women, but they have different implications. "Mrs." is typically used for a married woman who has taken her husband's last name. "Ms." is a more general title that can be used for any woman, regardless of marital status. 

Using "Ms." in written communication is often considered more appropriate when you don't know a woman's marital status, or if the woman prefers to not disclose it. "Ms." is also commonly used in professional or business settings, as it doesn't place emphasis on a woman's marital status, which is seen as irrelevant in such settings.
If hired, how much time would you need to give notice to your current employer? in chinese
如果被录用了，您需要多长时间通知您目前的雇主？
I'm currently unemployed. check grammar
The grammar in this sentence is correct.
I'm currently unemployed. make it formal
"I am presently not employed."
I'm currently unemployed. check spelling
The spelling in this sentence is correct.
I'm currently unemployed, I can start as soon as possible. check grammar

The grammar in this sentence is correct. However, for a more formal tone, you could rephrase it as:

"I am currently available to commence work immediately."
